Best Software

Best Electrical Construction Software (2026)

Electrical contractors juggle service work, residential installs, and commercial subcontracting — often all at once. The right software depends on which side of the business dominates.

✓ Updated April 2026

Electrical contractors face a unique software challenge: the tools built for field service (Jobber, ServiceTitan) are optimized for scheduling and dispatch but lack estimating depth. The tools built for construction (Procore, Buildertrend) handle large projects well but aren't designed for the rapid-turnover service side of electrical work. And then there's commercial electrical estimating, which is its own discipline entirely.

This guide breaks down the best software by the type of electrical work you primarily do.

Quick comparison

Software Starting Price Best For Key Strength
Knowify ~$149/mo Commercial electrical subcontractors AIA billing, job costing, contract management
Jobber $69/mo Residential electrical service Quoting, scheduling, invoicing
Contractor Foreman $49/mo Small electrical contractors, mixed work Value, estimating, scheduling
Buildertrend $499/mo Electrical contractors doing larger projects Full PM suite, client portal, subs
Accubid / Trimble Custom Commercial electrical estimating Assembly-based electrical takeoffs

1. Knowify — Best for commercial electrical subcontractors

Pricing: ~$149/mo starter; contact for Pro and Enterprise

For commercial electrical subcontractors — running projects under a GC, billing by application for payment, managing subcontracts and change orders — Knowify is purpose-built for your business model. Most construction PM platforms treat subcontractors as an afterthought; Knowify built its workflow around how subs actually operate.

Why commercial electricians use it:

  • AIA billing (G702/G703): Generate proper applications for payment automatically — a major time saver on commercial jobs
  • Job costing against bid: Track labor hours and material costs against your original estimate in real time
  • Change order management: Document, submit, and track change orders with GC approval workflow
  • Subcontract management: If you sub out low-voltage or other work, manage those contracts within the platform
  • Budget tracking: Know your actual margin throughout the project, not just at final close-out
  • QuickBooks sync: Export job costs and billing data to QuickBooks without double-entry

Limitations:

  • Not designed for service call scheduling and dispatch
  • Estimating module is general-purpose, not assembly-based electrical takeoff
  • Smaller user community than Procore or Buildertrend

2. Jobber — Best for residential electrical service

Pricing: Core $69/mo · Connect $169/mo · Grow $349/mo

For electrical contractors whose primary work is residential service — panel upgrades, EV charger installations, outlet and switch work, troubleshooting — Jobber provides the scheduling, quoting, and invoicing workflow without unnecessary complexity. It's the most efficient tool for companies doing 5–30 service calls per week.

Strengths for residential electricians:

  • Online quote requests and customer approval portal
  • Crew scheduling with drag-and-drop and mobile access
  • Time tracking in the field linked to specific jobs
  • Online payment collection with automatic follow-up reminders
  • Customer history — see what work was done at each address
  • Review request automation after job completion

Limitations:

  • No AIA billing or commercial contract management
  • Estimating is basic — works for simple service quotes, not complex commercial bids
  • Not built for projects lasting weeks or months

3. Contractor Foreman — Best for mixed electrical work

Pricing: Basic $49/mo · Standard $79/mo · Pro $159/mo · Unlimited $299/mo

If your electrical business does a mix of service work and small to mid-size installation projects, Contractor Foreman handles both more capably than pure service tools while costing far less than Buildertrend. Estimating, scheduling, time tracking, invoicing, and basic job costing are all included even at the Basic tier.

Good for: Small electrical contractors doing residential service plus panel changes, small commercial tenant improvements, and home additions — work that's too complex for Jobber but doesn't justify Buildertrend's price.

4. Buildertrend — For larger electrical installation projects

Pricing: Essential $499/mo · Advanced $799/mo · Complete $1,099/mo

If you're an electrical contractor doing large residential projects (full home wiring for custom builds, major renovations) where you need to coordinate with GCs, manage multiple crews over weeks, and handle client communication and change orders formally, Buildertrend provides project management depth that other options lack.

Buildertrend — When your electrical projects are complex enough to need full project management.

Visit Buildertrend →

5. Commercial electrical estimating: a separate category

Commercial electrical estimating — assembly-based takeoffs from drawings, with material pricing databases and labor unit calculations — is a specialized discipline that general construction PM tools don't handle well. If commercial estimating is critical to your business, look at dedicated electrical estimating software alongside your project management platform:

  • Accubid (Trimble): Industry standard for commercial electrical estimating. Assembly-based, integrates with electrical material pricing databases. Complex but comprehensive.
  • McCormick Estimating: Long-standing electrical estimating platform, popular with mid-size commercial electrical contractors.
  • ConEst: Strong for electrical estimating with labor unit databases built in.
  • Stack: More general-purpose takeoff tool, less electrical-specific but works for simpler commercial bids.

These specialized estimating tools typically pair with a separate project management platform (Knowify, Buildertrend, or Procore) for the actual job execution side.

Which software matches your electrical business?

Commercial electrical subcontracting (AIA billing, GC-managed projects): Knowify

Residential service calls and installs: Jobber

Mixed service and small projects, tight budget: Contractor Foreman

Large residential or light commercial projects: Buildertrend

Commercial estimating: Accubid, McCormick, or ConEst (dedicated tools)
